claim: Combination nicotine replacement therapies with varenicline or bupropion are not associated with significantly higher long-term abstinence rates at 52 weeks than varenicline monotherapy.

result.claim: Combination nicotine replacement therapies with varenicline or bupropion are not associated with significantly higher long-term abstinence rates at 52 weeks than varenicline monotherapy.
result.reasoning: The claim states that combination nicotine replacement therapies with varenicline or bupropion are not associated with significantly higher long-term abstinence rates at 52 weeks than varenicline monotherapy. However, evidence from a cohort study (Paper ID: 6b09cc9854c22b4bc12e5136a5b02b433585ac51, Credibility Rating: 2.8/5, Sample Size: 3569, Study Duration: 7 years) directly refutes this. This study, titled "Effectiveness of Varenicline versus Combination Nicotine Replacement Therapy for Smoking Cessation: One Year Outcomes in a Smoking Cessation Clinic in Taiwan," found that varenicline users had a significantly higher sustained abstinence rate at 52 weeks compared to those using combination nicotine replacement treatment (15.2% vs. 10.3%, p=0.001; aOR= 1.47, 95%CI: 1.05-2.05). This indicates that varenicline monotherapy was associated with *significantly higher* long-term abstinence rates than combination NRT, which contradicts the claim. While other papers discuss the effectiveness of various cessation aids, including varenicline, bupropion, and NRT, and some systematic reviews (e.g., Paper ID: 38997788, Credibility Rating: 5.0/5) mention general combination pharmacotherapy being more effective than usual care, none directly support the specific comparison and negative association stated in the claim for 52-week abstinence. The direct comparison in paper 6b09cc9854c22b4bc12e5136a5b02b433585ac51 is the most relevant and directly refutes the claim.

evidence.text: Data were available for varenicline, cytisine, bupropion and nicotine replacement therapy (NRT).
evidence.source: Effectiveness of smoking cessation interventions among adults: an overview of systematic reviews.

evidence.text: In one trial, three additional participants were abstinent at 2 years after receiving bupropion.
evidence.source: Effectiveness of smoking cessation interventions among adults: an overview of systematic reviews.

evidence.text: There were 101 more abstinent participants per 1000 in the varenicline group (95% CI: 29 to 209 more; single trial, 523 participants).
evidence.source: Effectiveness of smoking cessation interventions among adults: an overview of systematic reviews.

evidence.text: Varenicline, cytisine, bupropion, and nicotine replacement therapy (NRT) were all compared to placebo.
evidence.source: Effectiveness of smoking cessation interventions among adults: an overview of systematic reviews.

evidence.text: The Royal Australian College of General Practitioners (RACGP) recommends brief smoking cessation advice and pharmacotherapies (NRT, varenicline, bupropion).
evidence.source: Effectiveness of smoking cessation interventions among adults: an overview of systematic reviews.

evidence.text: Pharmaceutical interventions (i.e. varenicline, cytisine, nicotine replacement therapy (NRT), bupropion) showed increased smoking cessation.
evidence.source: Effectiveness of smoking cessation interventions among adults: an overview of systematic reviews.

evidence.text: Varenicline, cytisine, nicotine replacement therapy (NRT), and bupropion are pharmaceutical interventions that can increase smoking cessation.
evidence.source: Effectiveness of smoking cessation interventions among adults: an overview of systematic reviews.

evidence.text: 68 more participants per 1000 taking varenicline were point prevalence abstinent at 6-month follow-up compared to placebo.
evidence.source: Effectiveness of smoking cessation interventions among adults: an overview of systematic reviews.

evidence.text: Varenicline, cytisine, nicotine replacement therapy (NRT), and bupropion are pharmaceutical interventions that were evaluated.
evidence.source: Effectiveness of smoking cessation interventions among adults: an overview of systematic reviews.

evidence.text: For extended varenicline treatment (2 mg/day for 6–12 months) compared to placebo, 177 more per 1000 stopped smoking.
evidence.source: Effectiveness of smoking cessation interventions among adults: an overview of systematic reviews.

evidence.text: New Zealand’s Ministry of Health (2021) stipulates that brief advice (approximately 30 s), behavioural support, NRT, bupropion, varenicline and nortriptyline are the most effective cessation aids.
evidence.source: Effectiveness of smoking cessation interventions among adults: an overview of systematic reviews.

evidence.text: 68 more participants per 1000 taking varenicline were point prevalence abstinent at 6-month follow-up as compared to placebo among smokers with no immediate intention to quit.
evidence.source: Effectiveness of smoking cessation interventions among adults: an overview of systematic reviews.

evidence.text: 179 more per 1000 participants taking varenicline for 6 months were abstinent at 12 months compared to placebo.
evidence.source: Effectiveness of smoking cessation interventions among adults: an overview of systematic reviews.

evidence.text: More participants receiving varenicline (2 mg/day) were abstinent at 12 months follow-up.
evidence.source: Effectiveness of smoking cessation interventions among adults: an overview of systematic reviews.

evidence.text: Pharmacological interventions to aid smokers in cessation include nicotine replacement therapy, bupropion, and varenicline.
evidence.source: Smoking and Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD). Parallel Epidemics of the 21st Century

evidence.text: At longest follow-up (6+ months), 71 more people per 1000 were abstinent when receiving combination pharmacotherapy and behavioral support compared to those receiving usual care or minimal intervention.
evidence.source: Effectiveness of smoking cessation interventions among adults: an overview of systematic reviews.

evidence.text: The study aimed to compare the 12-month sustained abstinence rates of smokers using varenicline versus combination nicotine replacement treatment (cNRT) in their quit attempt.
evidence.source: Effectiveness of Varenicline versus Combination Nicotine Replacement Therapy for Smoking Cessation: One Year Outcomes in a Smoking Cessation Clinic in Taiwan.

evidence.text: The 2023 systematic review and network meta-analysis showed that nicotine patches, fast-acting nicotine, and bupropion were more effective than control.

evidence.text: The Royal Australian College of General Practitioners (RACGP) recommends bupropion for smoking cessation.

evidence.text: At 6-month follow-up, 66 more participants per 1000 on bupropion were abstinent.
